speaker: interviewer
text: 13, 1995, Tape three. Elizabeth you're going to go on and tell me they've put you in the barracks now at Auschwitz--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --and about the tattooing.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. After a couple of days. First of all, let me tell you the food there was horrible. Our first food was-- is black coffee and black hard bread. It was terrible tasting. And about two-- about two, three days after we'd been there, they took us for the tattooing. And my number is A6145. It was painfully.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did they take you to--?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was just girls were doing that, nothing special place. A table was set up.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the barrack?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, actually, I think it was outside, if I remember, or in a big room, if I could recall it. Remember now, this is 50 years ago. And after we get that, actually we lost our name. There was no more name--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who did the tattooing? Other Jewish--

speaker: interviewee
text: Other prisoners.

speaker: interviewer
text: Other prisoners?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Other prisoners, yes. It was kind of painful because they put the needle, they didn't put nothing on your arm, just put the needles in. And we lost our names, became a number. And after that, they got prison-- we were assigned to go to work. And we got a prison clothes, dress what was grey color and a white kerchief because our job was to work in the [NON-ENGLISH]. They called actually Kanada or [NON-ENGLISH]. We were marching like soldiers, and we had to march between the crematoriums. It was five crematoria in Auschwitz-Birkenau. And every morning and in the evening, we have to pass by. That's the only way we could reach our work place. This was a very, very large room. And as the transport were coming in, and they took their belongings away from there, and then they was putting into this large room and we were sorting these clothes, the clothes, the little shoes, the toothbrush, the jewelry, the food and, and whatever they brought along. And we wouldn't even try to take anything if he would find a piece of jewelry because if you got caught, that was the end of you. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anyone try and take anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody who I know. Someone would take some and trade it for piece of bread, or, or something. That's all, because that's all you wanted over there, just food. Because that was very terrible. And so we'd been working there, and many a times when we were walking, we would meet truckloads of naked girls who were driven-- or men-- riding to the crematorium. And by this time, we know what it was and what was going on. We learned very, very fast the living condition, what we came to. And a matter of fact, that same weekend, Sunday, when we were anxiously wanted to wait for our parents to meet. And one of the blockalteste who was there already many years--

speaker: interviewer
text: One of the what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Blockalteste who was taking care of the block, the girls. She had a little better position like we prisoners because they were there already longer. And we were the Hungarian Jews, and we were the last one who were coming in.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were very-- I said, when are we going to meet our family? Then she let us know that we never going to meet our family because do you see that big chimney there? She says they were gassed. You didn't even, you know, had your hair cut, they were already dead. So it was such a terrible, terrible feeling to knowing that you're never going to see them anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when this truckload of girls or men would drive into the crematorium, and we could hear their cry or scream. And then by the time we could pass by the crematorium, everything was quiet except the flames from the tall chimney. We could see and we could smell that horrible smell-- flesh burning, what was coming. And that we saw every single day, day as we were walking by. And we were working in this place till the transport was coming in. I, I believe maybe it stopped in September or something like that. I don't know-- we lost time over there. We didn't know what day it was, or what day. or month, or whatever. Sometime if you heard through the men, you know, the girls would hear some kind of news or anything like that. But otherwise, we just lost count. And after the transport were stopped coming, then we would have another job. Well, first of all, we had to get up every morning so early, about 3 o'clock in the morning. So we had very, very little sleep. And stay in the line till we were counted. And then Mengele would come and look over you, and see--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know it was Mengele?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, you-- you couldn't miss him at all because first of all, he was such a handsome man. He was dressed differently, and, and we know it was him. We saw him every--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did someone tell you it was him the first time?

speaker: interviewee
text: We saw him-- yes. We saw him every day. Matter of fact, he took my aunt away from the line one day when he was counting us. I don't know what was the reason that time, but she was able to get back. Matter of fact, she-- he took her away twice from the line as he was counting. And the food was so terrible, you know, over there that first of all, they put medication into food so they could dope us so we would--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know they put--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we know that because they had to put-- you know, the woman, they didn't have their period there. So they put medication. And then, I was reading that the girls who were working in the kitchen, they, they had to put in this white powder in the food.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were reading about this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: After?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. No, but we already know over there, because with able mind-- I mean with able mind, you couldn't do that what we saw. And just like if they would tell me walk into the crematorium, I would just walk in. I don't think I would just resist at all. During the night, I would wake up and go out to the washroom-- this was in Auschwitz-Birkenau-- before the crowd would come in.

speaker: interviewee
text: One thing over there, I tried to kept clean. Even it was cold in winter, but I would-- you know, we only had cold running water. It wasn't drinking water because it was bombed, and it was-- it was contaminated. And I would just stand there and look at the big flames, you know? And with, with normal mind, I don't think you would be able to do that. And many times, we hear the screaming, too.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so life was very, very, very hard over there. And after the transport stopped coming, they made us dig some ditches. And we were--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in Auschwitz-Birkenau, in the forest, in the forest. I don't know what they were gonna do. [INAUDIBLE] just keep us, I guess, busy, just to do something. They had a quota there, how many people they have to kill every day. Now, we were in the working labor camp, but then the other side, like the C lager, that's where the-- where the prisoners were where for selection every single day, how many people they were going to kill. Or example, or when you were sick, you wouldn't want them to know because then for sure you, you would be selected to go there. I remember a couple occasion, I was very sick. Especially at the beginning, when we got in there. We got in May. I think it must have been in June. I got awful sick there with very high temperature, and my throat was hurting me very, very badly. I couldn't swallow, and there was no medication or anything. And I really didn't care. I, I would have rather stayed in the barracks instead of going to work, but my sister wouldn't let me say there. She dragged me. She, she just wouldn't leave me there because she know wouldn't find me if I would be stayed in the camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I dragged along, and slowly I got, you know, better through somehow we find some Aspirin or something, what could take the fever off. Where did you find the Aspirin? Where did you get it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Actually, we met three cousins there at one time, and we were walking home from work. My aunt, who escaped from Czechoslovakia, she recognized them. That was a very interesting thing too because I didn't know them, but they lived in the same place. So one of my cousin was able to get some medication so I--

speaker: interviewer
text: But you didn't know how he got it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know how she got it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, she.

speaker: interviewee
text: But she-- that's the very, very first time we met. And when this was already in September end of October. One day, we were working on the ditch, and we were out of the camp. And all of a sudden the siren start to blow, you know. And we heard a big explosion, but we didn't know what it was. And of course, right away we had to get into the camp. The German came with their dog and everything. And we get into the camp, and we had to stand up in line five in a row. That was so-- every single day, that was the most important thing for them to do, the counting. And we find out that what happened was-- is these people, the Sonderkommando who were working in the crematorium, the men's. They were working only there two months, and then they got killed too.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when their time came, they really didn't care what happened, because they were going to be killed anyway, so it didn't matter. So some girls, somehow, they managed to get them some explosives to them, and they blew up one of the crematorium, I think the number four crematorium. Did you know any of the people?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. I didn't know. But because of that, all the prisoner had to stay in line all the time till, of course, they were captured. They couldn't go no place because they put electric wire around us. And, of course, they were killed, and they-- they shoot them. And the girls who bought them the explosives, they were-- later they were punished, too. They were hanged.

speaker: interviewer
text: Those were the Jewish prisoners?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish prisoners, yes. So that one of the things happened while we were in Auschwitz-Birkenau. Then I remember one night we were walking home, and this SS man-- every other person, he give a punch, a slap on the face. And, of course, my sister was on the other line, and she got one, and she lost two teeth. Two teeth fell out, she started to bleed, and she had a very swollen face. And I cried for her, and she told me, I says I shouldn't cry. She said I'm-- I feel like a soldier, and I says I have to act like one. And we just marched on.

speaker: interviewer
text: So those things, you know, it's, it's very painful, even after 50 years to talk about it. Then, in November-- yes, this was in November. It was getting cold already, but we were still there in Braunschweig-- I mean in Birkenau. And we were going back from lunch, and instead of going on the pavement, I was going cutting through the grass. And I didn't know this SS man was watching me from the window. And he comes out, and he calls me back. And he asked me, why did I step on the grass? So I just told him, you know, I was-- wanted to go back on time to work. And no question asked, he just took out his rubber stick, and he started to hit me. He hit me so hard on my back, and I was running away from him. I didn't care if he was going to shoot me or whatever. I was running, and he ran after me as long as he could, but I guess I outrun him. And after I get back to my work place, the pain was so bad that I just couldn't open up my mouth, you know? I couldn't say a word. It just went to my heart, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: This, this happened over there. And end of November, not knowing what was happening around us. Of course, airplanes and shooting, that we could always hear. And one night, I think it must have been after midnight, this SS man comes into the barrack, and he says they're looking for girls to take away for working. Now, we wasn't sure if it was really work, or if they were selecting to go to the gas chamber. So we ran to the next block, the next hut first. Then somehow or another, we got word from our cousin. She was working in a place where she know which transport is really going away, and which not. And she told us we should take this transport. This will go away.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went back to our regular block where we were. Where was she working that she knew this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where the transport was really going away, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she was right there where they were going?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like at the station?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And so we signed up, then they took us in again. We had to shower, and we had to take our clothes off. And two line of SS men was there, and we had to walk between the two line of SS men. On the very end was Mengele. So we passed, and I remember we were pinching our face so we should look good and hearty. And you even smiled, you know? It was nothing to smile for, but you wanted to be, you know, cordial or, you know, look you are happy or whatever. And my sister passed. I passed. My other sister, [NON-ENGLISH], she came. My aunt was the last in the row. And we looked back, and she wasn't coming. Mengele took her out because she had some rash on her stomach. And after that, we didn't know what happened to her. We never saw her anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the next day, all day, we had to stay in line and wait, and see. And we were hesitated. We were wondering if this transport really going to go away or not. Finally, they let us get into the cattle cars, many girls in one of those cars. And they locked the door. And we got a pail and very little bread. And not even water because you couldn't drink the water there. I mean, you just wouldn't. I remember, I think it was just a portion what we used to get, the food every day, that's all we got. And we were just sitting there.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the meantime, we could hear-- the-- this was-- the train was stationed between the crematorium. So we could hear the closing of the doors as they slammed the doors. We could hear the scream, then we could hear the quietness. You know, everything quiet. And we could smell the flesh, the burning flesh.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it must have been about after midnight. All of a sudden the train started to move. And everybody had a big sigh that, oh my god, we're really going away from this hell. This place was like hell, you know? For us. And we did go, and the train took us to Bergen-Belsen. And that was another death camp. We didn't know at that time. We thought we were going to a better place to work.

speaker: interviewee
text: But you know, if you don't work, they hardly give you anything to eat. And we were there for a few weeks, I can recall how long. We were getting very weak and, and sick.

speaker: interviewer
text: They put you in barracks there, too?

speaker: interviewee
text: Barracks, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wooden barracks?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wooden barracks, and three levels of bed again. And my sister, we said, well let's-- I hope they're going to select some people to go away from here to go to work, because this is really not a place to be. And sure enough, one day they were-- came, the SS men, and says, they needed girl to go to work. So we very gladly volunteered to go. And we signed up, and they were getting us ready to, you know, to the transport.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my sister got very, very sick that time. She developed diarrhea, and she was fainting the whole day. And here, they are counting us all the time. And we realized, I says, my god, what are we going to do? So we find something for her to sit on. And when the SS men want to come, they're going to make her stand up for a few minutes. Then he goes by and make her sit down again. And this been going on the whole day like that. It was just a terrible, terrible thing. And that time already, I didn't have my aunt with me anymore. So I took-- What happened to her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they took her away when we left Birkenau. Mengele took her away, and then we didn't know what happened to her. And I took my sister [NON-ENGLISH]. I was acting like a mother for her this time, and I took care of her. We didn't have another underwear or thing to change. I cleaned her up. I give mine to her to wear, and I washed as good I can in cold water, hers. And I put it on my body to have it dried up from the body heat as much as it could. And finally, the time came, and we went to the train again, the cattle train. We kind of was glad to see the cattle train already, to get in there. And we were so, so hungry. So I saw some grass, and I pulled some grass. And I was eating it, and I gave some for my sister to have some, too. And I don't know if that made her feel better or whatever, but once the train stopped moving from Bergen-Belsen, somehow she felt, you know, like a different person. And from Bergen-Belsen, we went to Braunschweig. That was a bombed city. It's only us prisoner were living there, and of course, the SS men who was watching us. There was no other civilians or any other life over there. And our home was a stable, and that was full of hay. That's where our bed was. And the very, very first night we slept in, we were infested with lice very quickly all over our body, our clothes, and it was just, just so terrible, you know. After that, we couldn't keep ourself clean anymore. I developed boils on my thigh, and the dirty clothes was just sticking to it, you know. And I don't know how to rip it off. So the whole, whatever, crust, you know, came. I still bear the sign where it was. And my knees, it was so swollen. It was very hard to work. We were cleaning up the rubbles-- the rubble from what they were bombed.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you fed there?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you fed?

speaker: interviewee
text: We got the food what we usually got-- black coffee, bread, and soup for one-- for one meal. All kind of junk was in that soup. It was heavier than water, let's put it that way. But it looked like dirt. And if-- I was reading later on what they were putting into to the food, you know, what kind of a thing. It's amazing that we survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: But at the time, you didn't know really what it was.

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't know what it was, but we did know it was terrible. Over there, too, was very cold. It was winter time. Our clothing was very limited. It wasn't-- you know, I mean, we weren't dressed for winter. And one time, it was an air raid. That meant we had to hide. And one of the house was just the four walls standing, and that's where we went in. And because of the airplanes and the shooting, the walls started to sway. And I got so scared. I thought everything is going to fall on us, and we going to get killed.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was going to run out, and my sister catched me, and hold me so tight to her chest that I shouldn't run away. Otherwise, I probably would have been killed. I wouldn't be able to tell you this story. But she saved my life so many times. And so every camp, wherever we went, it has a different story. So we were here for a while, and then they were moving us someplace else.
